ontology.foodon_gap_note: Specific dish 'Palak Paneer Set' not found in FoodOn; closest parent is the Indian cuisine food product class. A more specific term for palak paneer or composite Indian set meal does not exist in the current FoodOn release.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: Specific FoodEx2 code for 'Palak Paneer Set' does not exist; A0171 (mixed dish, Indian style) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT does not contain a specific concept for Palak Paneer Set; 226020007 (Indian food) is the closest available parent concept.

serving.natural_language_portion: One set meal typically consists of a portion of palak paneer, dal tadka, saffron pulao, and butter naan, suitable for a single adult serving.

nutrient_source.title: Palak Paneer Set
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient data directly from HPB Singapore for 'Palak Paneer Set'.

gi_source.title: Spinach (raw), Paneer, Dal (lentils), Basmati Rice (cooked), Naan (plain) — component-based analogous estimate
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI estimated based on primary components sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database: Spinach (very low GI ~15), Paneer (very low GI ~15), Dal/lentils (low GI ~29), Basmati Rice cooked (medium GI ~58), Naan plain (medium-high GI ~71). The composite dish GI is estimated at 50 (low) reflecting the high proportion of low-GI ingredients (spinach, paneer, dal) offsetting the higher-GI rice and naan components. GL calculation: (9.86g carb per 100g × 350g serving / 100) × 50 / 100 = 17.25.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content at 311mg/100g is borderline elevated; potassium (247mg/100g) and dietary fibre (2.8g/100g) provide partial offset.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced salt and cream during preparation to manage sodium and saturated fat intake, and monitor total meal sodium contributed by accompaniments such as rice and naan.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low sugar (1.01g/100g) and moderate carbohydrate (9.86g/100g) with good protein (7.05g/100g) and fibre (2.8g/100g) support glycaemic control, but the set includes higher-GI components such as naan and rice.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consider requesting a smaller portion of rice or substituting with roti to reduce glycaemic load, and avoid added cream which increases caloric density without glycaemic benefit.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat estimated at 4.2g/100g is near the threshold of concern, and cholesterol is 19mg/100g; spinach and lentils provide beneficial dietary fibre and antioxidants.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: To reduce cardiovascular risk, individuals should request reduced cream or butter in preparation to lower saturated fat content, and monitor overall fat intake across all meal components.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Spinach is rich in anti-inflammatory antioxidants (vitamin C, vitamin K, folate); turmeric and ginger in the spice base provide curcumin and gingerols; calcium (235mg/100g) supports bone health.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should monitor their individual response to nightshade vegetables such as tomatoes if present, and request reduced cream or butter to lower saturated fat content.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Palak Paneer Set is a popular North Indian vegetarian meal served as a complete set, typically comprising Palak Paneer (a creamy spinach and Indian cottage cheese curry), Dal Tadka (tempered yellow lentil soup), Saffron Pulao (fragrant basmati rice cooked with saffron), and Butter Naan (soft leavened flatbread). The Palak Paneer component is characterised by its vibrant green colour and velvety texture, with soft cubes of paneer providing a mild, milky contrast to the spiced spinach gravy. Dal Tadka offers a comforting, savoury, and lightly spiced lentil base enriched with a tempering of cumin, garlic, and dried chilli. Saffron Pulao adds an aromatic, subtly sweet dimension, while Butter Naan is pillowy and slightly charred, ideal for scooping curries. Nutritionally, this set provides a good source of protein (7.05g/100g) from paneer and lentils, dietary fibre (2.8g/100g) from spinach and lentils, and carbohydrates (9.86g/100g) from rice and naan. It is notably rich in calcium (235mg/100g) and magnesium (62mg/100g) from paneer and spinach, and iron (1.74mg/100g) from spinach and lentils. Sodium is borderline at 311mg/100g. Clinically, the dish is suitable for most adults when portion sizes of rice and naan are moderated, and added cream or butter is minimised, particularly for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease.
common_user_queries: Nutritional value of Indian set meal?

confidence_reason: Nutrient data is directly sourced from HPB Singapore for 'Palak Paneer Set'. GI is estimated from primary components using the University of Sydney GI Database, which is an approved GI source. Saturated fat is estimated based on typical paneer and cream content in such a dish.
notes: Monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown, micronutrient, and moisture data for this composite dish are not individually reported in the HPB Singapore database for this record. Saturated_fat_g (4.2g/100g) is estimated based on typical paneer and cream content in a palak paneer preparation; this value is not explicitly reported by HPB for this record.

Minor improvements to LLM description for additional clinical specificity.
